learn.title: Finding the Best Wind Sites
learn.intro: Wind resource assessment guides developers to sites with steady, strong winds, cutting energy costs and boosting output. Poor siting wastes millions and harms grids. IRENA shows how mapping wind speed, turbulence and terrain ensures turbines earn maximum clean power for decades.
learn.c1_text: Developers use met masts and LiDAR to record wind at 10–120 m height for a year. Like checking a river’s flow before building a mill, they confirm average speed exceeds 6 m/s for viable turbines.
learn.c2_text: GIS layers combine wind data with terrain, roads and grids. A 7 m/s site yields nearly double the energy of a 5 m/s one, because power rises with the cube of speed, shaping final project layouts.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Institute of Wind Energy estimates 695 GW potential at 120 m hub height. Gujarat and Tamil Nadu host large farms after assessment showed coastal corridors with 2500+ full-load hours yearly.
learn.stat1: India wind potential: 695 GW at 120 m (NIWE)
learn.stat2: Wind power cubed: 7 m/s gives ~2x of 5 m/s
learn.stat4: Tamil Nadu wind capacity: over 10 GW

learn.title: Solar PV and Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Solar panels turn sunlight directly into electricity via the photoelectric effect, powering homes without emissions. Understanding this helps citizens adopt clean energy and cut bills. US DOE explains how silicon cells unlock the planet’s most abundant resource for the transition.
learn.c1_text: Sunlight photons strike a silicon cell and knock electrons loose. Like a cue ball scattering pins, one photon frees one electron, creating a tiny current when metal contacts collect them at about 0.5 V per cell.
learn.c2_text: 60 cells wire in series to make a 300 W panel. An inverter changes their DC to AC. A 1 kW rooftop system in India yields ~4 kWh daily, enough to run a fridge and fans.
learn.c3_text: India crossed 90 GW solar in 2024, with 12 GW rooftop. PM Surya Ghar scheme gives subsidies up to ₹78,000, pushing household PV using the same photoelectric principle nationwide.
learn.stat3: One photon frees one electron in silicon

learn.title: Demand Response Balances Grid
learn.intro: Demand response lets big users cut or shift load when grids are stressed, avoiding blackouts and coal peakers. It is cheaper than new wires. US DOE shows how factories and malls become flexible partners in a renewable-heavy, variable power system.
learn.c1_text: A textile plant pauses machines during evening peaks, like delaying laundry to off-peak hours. It earns payment per kW reduced; US programs hit 30 GW shifted, equal to 60 mid-size plants.
learn.c2_text: Smart meters receive price or reliability signals. Buildings pre-cool at noon using solar surplus, then coast through peak. This shaves demand spikes and integrates 100+ GW renewables smoothly.
learn.c3_text: DISCOMs in Delhi and Maharashtra run industrial DR saving 500–1000 MW in summers. Smart meters under RDSS enable 250 million connections for future automated response by 2025.
learn.stat3: Delhi DR saves up to 1000 MW summer
learn.stat4: DR cost: lower than new peaker plants

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage Basics
learn.intro: Pumped hydro is the world’s largest battery, storing excess solar and wind as gravity potential. It stabilizes grids cheaply for hours. Though IRENA source mislabels biofuels, the technology remains key: 90% of global storage is this water-based giant.
learn.c1_text: Surplus power pumps water uphill to a high lake. Like charging a phone, it stores energy; releasing flows down through turbines generating ~80% of the input back, far better than many batteries.
learn.c2_text: Reversible pump-turbines switch modes in minutes. A 1000 MW plant can run 10 hours, delivering 10 GWh. This buffers a day of calm wind better than lithium at utility scale.
learn.c3_text: India has 4.7 GW pumped hydro and targets 50 GW by 2030. Tehri and Kadamparai plants already balance northern and southern grids with large reservoirs.

learn.title: Rooftop Solar and Net Metering
learn.intro: Rooftop solar lets homes sell extra power to the grid via net metering, cutting bills and emissions. MNRE India drives adoption through subsidies. This decentralised generation is vital for resilient cities and energy access in the transition.
learn.c1_text: Rooftop PV makes DC power; an inverter sends AC to your home. Like a personal well, it feeds you first, then exports surplus at ~1 kW per 10 sq m of panel.
learn.c2_text: Your meter spins backward when exporting. Monthly bill counts only net import. A 3 kW system in Mumbai can zero a ₹2000 bill and earn credits at ~₹3 per kWh surplus.
learn.c3_text: MNRE’s PM Surya Ghar gives 40% subsidy on ≤3 kW and 20% up to 10 kW. By 2024, India reached 12 GW rooftop, aiming 40 GW by 2026 under this scheme.

learn.title: Green Steel via Hydrogen
learn.intro: Steel emits 8% of CO2; green hydrogen can replace coal in blast furnaces to decarbonise. IEA roadmap shows direct reduction using H2 yields near-zero emissions. This is critical for clean construction and India’s industrial rise.
learn.c1_text: Traditional blast furnaces burn coke to remove oxygen from iron ore. Using H2 instead, like swapping a charcoal grill for hydrogen flame, yields water not CO2, cutting emissions up to 95%.
learn.c2_text: Hydrogen direct reduction iron (DRI) reacts H2 with Fe2O3 at 800°C to pure iron plus steam. Electrolysers need 50 kWh per kg H2; a 1 Mt plant uses ~150 MW renewable power steadily.
learn.c3_text: India, 2nd largest steel maker at 140 Mt, launched National Green Hydrogen Mission with ₹19,700 crore. NMDC and JSW plan H2-DRI pilots by 2025 to cut 250 Mt CO2 sector-wide.
learn.stat2: H2 DRI cuts emissions up to 95%
